Military Mar 24, 2026

How The Fairchild Republic A-10 Warthog’s Titanium Bathtub Protects Pilots From Ground-Level Ballistics

The story behind America's flying tank buster.

The A-10's titanium armor cockpit design remains a gold standard for survivability engineering, prioritizing pilot protection over aerodynamic efficiency in hostile low-altitude combat environments.

See Simple Flying →

Aerospace Mar 24, 2026

NASA PC-12 Aircraft Makes Move to Support Flight Research Across Agency

A NASA Pilatus PC-12 aircraft will now be based at NASA’s Armstrong Flight Research Center in Edwards, California, in order to support flight research efforts across the agency. The PC-12 was acquired in 2022 by NASA’s Glenn Research Center in Cleveland for use in advanced technology development....

NASA's relocation of its PC-12 to Armstrong expands distributed research capabilities by positioning the aircraft at a premier flight test facility, enabling faster iteration on agency-wide technology demonstrations.

See NASA →

Commercial Mar 24, 2026

Delta suspends airport perks for members of US Congress as shutdown strains TSA

Delta Air Lines said on March 24, 2026, that it is temporarily suspending special services for members of… The post Delta suspends airport perks for members of US Congress as shutdown strains TSA appeared first on AeroTime.

Delta's suspension of congressional perks signals how operational disruptions from TSA staffing shortages now force airlines to reduce premium services, potentially affecting political relationships critical to aviation policy.

See AeroTime Hub →

Commercial Mar 24, 2026

Air Transat Scraps New 12-Hour Longest Route Before A Single Flight Takes Off

The leisure operator was due to take off from Toronto to Ghana's capital on June 27. But the route has been pulled before the first departure...

Air Transat's route cancellation signals that even pre-launch demand forecasting may miss critical profitability thresholds on ultra-long leisure routes.

See Simple Flying →

How Much Does It Cost To Upgrade To Lie-Flat Business Class On Transatlantic Flights?

Booking business class isn't the only way to access lie-flat transatlantic aviation. Most major transatlantic airlines offer upgrades.

Airlines strategically price mid-journey upgrades to capture additional revenue from premium economy passengers without discounting business class inventory.

Geopolitical shifts bring opportunities and headaches for satellite execs

A shifting geopolitical landscape is driving huge business opportunities for satcoms but also bringing new supply chain and regulatory challenges, industry executives said March 24 at the Satellite Conference in Washington, D.C. The post Geopolitical shifts bring opportunities and headaches for s...

Satellite operators must navigate competing regulatory frameworks and supply chain reshoring demands while capitalizing on geopolitical tensions driving increased satcom demand.

NASA leadership provided updates on mission priorities, including sending t...

NASA's display of lunar samples alongside announcements of transformative initiatives signals concrete progress toward renewed Moon exploration while establishing strategic space leadership priorities.

Safety Mar 24, 2026

The Evolution of Aviation SMS

From the Wright brothers to the 2024 FAA rule — how aviation safety evolved from reactive investigation to proactive risk management.

Modern SMS transforms aviation from investigating accidents after they occur to identifying and mitigating hazards before incidents happen, fundamentally changing industry safety culture.

Safety Mar 24, 2026

No Transponder, No Warning: NTSB Details LaGuardia Alert Failure

NTSB says LaGuardia’s surface warning system was "unable to alert."

LaGuardia's failure to detect a transponder-equipped aircraft suggests systemic gaps in surface safety infrastructure beyond equipment standards.

See Simple Flying →

Regulatory Mar 24, 2026

FAA Expands Swift 100R Engine STC

Amended STC broadens engine coverage for the unleaded avgas.

The expanded STC enables more aircraft models to safely operate on Swift 100R, accelerating the industry's transition away from leaded fuel.

See AVweb →

Aerospace Mar 24, 2026

3 Ways Students Can Get Involved With Artemis

NASA’s Artemis program will establish a sustainable lunar presence, unlock new scientific discoveries, and develop technologies for spaceflight to Mars and beyond – and students can help shape this new era of space exploration. As America launches this new Golden Age of innovation and exploration...

Students can directly contribute to NASA's lunar and Mars missions through Artemis engagement opportunities, positioning aerospace careers at the forefront of deep-space exploration technology development.

The San Francisco Bay area is complicated airspace, and right in the middle of it is San Carlos Airport. San Carlos airport is surrounded by terrain, San Francisco International class bravo, Metro-Oak

San Carlos Airport's noise abatement procedures require pilots to navigate complex terrain and nearby Class B airspace while maintaining community relations.

Multi-factor accident causation patterns suggest systemic vulnerabilities at major hubs that single-point mitigations may not fully address.

Industry Mar 24, 2026

NASA Selects Finalists in Student Aircraft Maintenance Competition

Editor’s note: This advisory was updated on March 24, 2026, to update the finalists list. NASA has selected seven student teams as finalists in the 2026 Gateways to Blue Skies Competition, giving them the resources to help address a critical challenge for U.S. aviation: aircraft maintenance. Chal...

NASA's aircraft maintenance competition targets workforce development in a sector facing severe technician shortages that directly impact airline operations and safety.

See NASA →

Drones Mar 24, 2026

ResilienX Receives FAA Waiver for Remote BVLOS Operations

ResilienX, Inc., a leader in aviation safety and BVLOS drone networks, announced today that it

FAA waivers for remote BVLOS operations enable drone operators to conduct inspections and monitoring across infrastructure without line-of-sight constraints, expanding commercial applications beyond visual range limitations.

See sUAS News →

Safety Mar 24, 2026

Flying in the Unforgiving Wire Environment (The Rotorcraft Collective)

Many pilots mistakenly believe they will have sufficient reaction time if they encounter power lines and other wires. Yet wire strikes have been among the top three causes of fatal helicopter accident

Wire strike accidents result from pilots significantly overestimating their reaction time in helicopter operations, making proactive avoidance planning essential rather than reactive maneuvering.

See Federal Aviation Administration (YouTube) →

Drones Mar 24, 2026

When Wireless Networks Falter, Drones Can Provide Backup Connectivity, According to Stevens Researchers

Researchers at Stevens Institute of Technology have developed a drone-based system designed to provide temporary

Autonomous drone networks can restore critical communications within hours of infrastructure failure, offering rapid disaster response capabilities for emergency services and isolated communities.

See sUAS News →

Drones Mar 24, 2026

America’s new 911 drone is always ready

Drones responding to 911 calls are no longer experimental. Across the US, police departments are already using Drone as First Responder (DFR) programs to get eyes on emergencies faster than patrol units. In many cities, drones arrive in under two minutes, well before officers. But even with that ...

Emergency response drones arriving in under two minutes is reshaping first-responder protocols and creating new operational standards that traditional patrol units cannot match.

See DroneDJ →

Air Cargo Mar 24, 2026

How Much Do Cargo Pilots Make Compared To Passenger Airline Pilots?

Passenger airline pilots can earn more, but being a cargo pilot is also a lucrative career.

Cargo pilots increasingly earn competitive salaries rivaling passenger carriers, making freight operations an attractive alternative career path amid passenger pilot supply constraints.

See Simple Flying →

Industry Mar 24, 2026

Towards Environmentally Sustainable Aviation: A Review on Operational Optimization

Growing air traffic is straining airports and airspace while raising environmental concerns, and demand is only expected to increase. This paper reviews how advanced technologies and methods can expand capacity and improve efficiency across airline, airport, and flight operations. It also highlights emerging trends and gaps for future research in sustainable aviation.

Operational optimization offers immediate emissions and capacity gains without waiting for alternative fuels or new aircraft, presenting a critical near-term lever for airlines to meet sustainability targets.

See MDPI →

Aerospace Mar 24, 2026

NASA X-Ray Mission Gets Fresh Look at 2,000-Year-Old Supernova

NASA’s IXPE (Imaging X-ray Polarimetry Explorer) mission has taken a new observation of a supernova, RCW 86, helping fill in a fuller picture of what other telescopes have observed. When astronomers using NASA’s Chandra X-ray Observatory previously targeted RCW 86, they discovered that a large “c...

IXPE's polarimetry data reveals magnetic field structures in ancient supernovae that traditional X-ray imaging cannot detect, enhancing aerospace science capabilities.

See NASA →

Regulatory Mar 24, 2026

SparkPNT Enters the Next Chapter with Facet FP

Designed with the belief that users should have total control over the devices they own,

Right-to-repair principles are expanding into aviation systems, challenging traditional manufacturer dependencies in critical infrastructure like positioning and navigation.

Aerospace Mar 24, 2026

Moog taps Redwire to provide solar arrays for Meteor

WASHINGTON — Redwire announced the first award for its new solar arrays, a $12.8 million contract from Moog. Under the contract announced March 24, Redwire will deliver Extensible Low-Profile Solar Array (ELSA) wings for Moog’s Meteor satellite bus ordered by an undisclosed national security cust...

Redwire's ELSA technology entering production for national security satellites signals growing commercial demand for deployable solar solutions in government space platforms.

See SpaceNews →

Safety Mar 24, 2026

NTSB: LaGuardia Surface Alert System Did Not Trigger

Investigators said the fire truck involved did not have a transponder, and a stepped-on radio transmission remains under review.

The lack of transponder equipment on ground vehicles represents a critical gap in airport surface detection systems that could enable future runway incursion incidents.

See AVweb →

Regulatory Mar 24, 2026

IAA Prosecutes Drone Operator for Impeding Investigation and Breach of Direction

The Irish Aviation Authority (IAA) brought a prosecution in the District Court against a drone operator for impeding an official investigation

The IAA's prosecution sets precedent that drone operators face criminal liability for obstructing regulatory investigations, not just operational violations.

See sUAS News →

Commercial Mar 24, 2026

Here’s Why Airlines Keep Refurbishing Boeing 777-300ER Cabins Instead Of Replacing Them

Airlines refurbish their Boeing 777-300ER cabins as it offers a more cost-effective solution and improves the efficiency of an aircraft's lifecycle.

Extended cabin refurbishment cycles for 777-300ERs signal airlines' confidence in the aircraft's structural longevity while deferring massive capital expenditures amid fleet modernization pressures.

Space-based IoT connectivity breakthroughs could enable airlines to implement real-time aircraft tracking and predictive maintenance systems beyond traditional ground networks.

FAA Updates GNSS Interference Guide Months After First Release

Update adds pilot procedures and expands detail on recognizing and reporting jamming and spoofing.

The updated guidance now equips pilots with actionable procedures to identify and report GNSS threats in real-time, closing a critical gap between detection and response in the field.

Instead, they point to […] The post From missions to systems: The architecture enabling a sustained lunar economy a...

Industry is shifting focus from individual lunar missions to integrated infrastructure systems, requiring aerospace suppliers to develop reusable, interconnected solutions for sustained commercial operations.

Aerospace Mar 24, 2026

China’s Astronstone raises $29 million for reusable rocket with chopstick-style recovery

Astronstone, one of China’s younger launch startups, has secured new funding as it builds toward the first flight of its reusable AS-1 rocket. The post China’s Astronstone raises $29 million for reusable rocket with chopstick-style recovery appeared first on SpaceNews.

Astronstone's chopstick-style recovery mechanism represents an alternative reusability approach competing with SpaceX's landing leg method, diversifying global reusable rocket technology development.
